summary: UR188 is a CADASTRAL SURVEY held at the MUSEE DU QUAI BRANLY, Paris -- first of the QUAI BRANLY TRIO (UR188 + UR189 + UR192, co-catalogued at the same museum). 78 cords, 1-PAIRE depth L2, 16 clusters, 5 colors AB(28 36%) + W(25 32%) + GG(11 14%) + MB(9 12%) + KB(5 6%). 8 STRING at 100 percent dict hit -- OVERWHELMINGLY GEOGRAPHIC: kaqa x3 (WALL, Aymara) = THE MOST WALL-FOCUSED KHIPU PER TEXT CORD IN THE ANALYSED CORPUS, plus qaqa x2 (rock landmarks), piqa x1 (summit / who-TOPIC), paqa x1 (east orientation), waqa x1 (HUACA -- sacred site within territory). 5 different geographic terms in 8 text cords = 62.5 PERCENT GEOGRAPHIC VOCABULARY concentration -- PURE CADASTRAL document. GG (grey-green justice/identity color, 14 percent) may mark boundaries with legal force -- walls that are juridically separative, not merely physical. OKR: C53 'tied loosely around main cord' = movable marker, possibly a physical bookmark left by the surveyor.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  78 cords, depth L2.
  Architecture: 1-PAIRE, 16 clusters.
  Colors: AB(28, 36%), W(25, 32%), GG(11, 14%), MB(9, 12%), KB(5, 6%). 5 colors.
  8 STRING (10 percent, 100 percent dict hit), 66 INT, 4 EMPTY.
  Provenance: UNKNOWN.
  Museum: Musee du Quai Branly, Paris -- CO-CATALOGUED WITH UR189 + UR192 (Quai Branly trio).
  OKR: C53 'tied loosely around main cord' = movable marker.

COLOR-AS-ROLE
  AB (light brown, 36%): PRIMARY SURVEY ENTRIES -- the land itself.
  W  (white, 32%): SECONDARY ENTRIES -- neutral data / reference.
  GG (grey-green, 14%): JUSTICE / IDENTITY COLOR -- boundaries with legal force (juridical walls).
  MB (medium brown, 12%): TERTIARY CATEGORY.
  KB (khaki brown, 6%): MINOR CATEGORY.
  GG at 14 percent is unusually prominent for a cadastral register -- suggests that boundaries in
  this survey carry juridical authority (legally contestable, not merely descriptive).

KEY VOCABULARY (8 STRING, 100 percent dict hit)
  kaqa x3 -- WALL (Aymara) = CORPUS RECORD for wall-focused concentration per text cord
  qaqa x2 -- ROCK landmarks
  piqa x1 -- SUMMIT / head / who-TOPIC (high point of terrain)
  paqa x1 -- EAST orientation
  waqa x1 -- HUACA (sacred site)
  5 geographic terms / 8 text cords = 62.5 percent geographic concentration. PURE CADASTRAL.

STRUCTURE
  78 cords in 16 clusters = ~4.9 cords per cluster.
  L2 depth (paired subsidiaries) = boundary section > plot entries.
  66 INT cords (85 percent) = numerical measurements (areas, distances, tribute values).
  4 EMPTY cords = section separators.
  8 STRING cords (10 percent) = the vocabulary mapping the terrain.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR188 is a WALL-FOCUSED CADASTRAL SURVEY. The kaqa x3 record concentration + waqa sacred anchor
  + piqa summit + paqa east + qaqa rocks = a territory defined primarily by its WALLS. GG 14 percent
  suggests juridical weight on the wall boundaries. C53 movable-marker is a surveyor's physical
  bookmark.
narrative:
Three walls. That is what defines this survey -- the Aymara word kaqa, three times, more than any other khipu in the corpus per text cord. A document whose land is defined by what encloses it rather than by what is inside it.

Between the walls: rocks -- qaqa, twice -- as landmarks anchoring the interior. A summit -- piqa -- a high point of terrain. The eastern direction -- paqa -- the orientation that every Inca cadastre carries. And a huaca -- waqa -- the sacred place that anchors the survey to the ritual landscape. Five geographic terms in eight text cords: the language of territory distilled.

Cord fifty-three was tied loosely around the main cord, a movable marker. Left by the surveyor, or by the investigator who later catalogued the khipu, as a bookmark in a register of terrain. Movable because its position did not encode a value -- it was a navigation aid, telling the reader where to resume consultation.

At the Musee du Quai Branly in Paris, this khipu rests alongside UR189 and UR192 -- the Quai Branly trio. Three Peruvian khipus co-catalogued at one Paris institution, acquired together through the 19th or 20th-century collecting networks. Their specific provenance is unknown but their museum co-location suggests a shared acquisition history.

Seventy-eight cords. Sixteen clusters. Five colours. The grey-green cords -- eleven of them, fourteen percent of the document -- carry the justice and identity function. These mark boundaries that have legal force, walls that are not merely physical but juridical, separating one community's land from another. The light brown and white cords carry the measurements: sixty-six numerical entries quantifying the terrain that the eight text cords name.

A summit to the east, rocks as landmarks, a huaca as the spiritual centre, and three walls enclosing the whole. This is a land survey in the fullest Andean sense, where geography and the sacred are the same thing, and where the boundaries carry the weight of law.

prior_work.claim: The Musee du Quai Branly in Paris holds a set of Peruvian khipus including UR188, UR189, UR192 -- the Quai Branly trio -- acquired through European collecting networks with partial provenance documentation.
prior_work.citation: Musee du Quai Branly, Paris catalog.

prior_work.claim: The Aymara term kaqa (wall) is attested across coastal and central-coast Inca documents; its prevalence marks the bilingual Quechua-Aymara contact zone.
prior_work.citation: Bertonio, L. (1612). Vocabulario de la lengua Aymara; Cerron-Palomino, R. (2000). Linguistica Aymara.

prior_work.claim: Andean cadastral surveys routinely incorporate huacas (waqa) as anchoring features -- the sacred + geographic are a single category in Inca territorial administration.
prior_work.citation: Zuidema, R. T. (1964). The Ceque System of Cuzco; Rostworowski, M. (1999). Historia del Tahuantinsuyu.

v3_contribution.claim: CORPUS RECORD WALL CONCENTRATION: kaqa x3 out of 8 STRING cords = 37.5 percent of text on a single wall-term. No other khipu in the analysed corpus concentrates this densely on kaqa per text cord.
62.5 PERCENT GEOGRAPHIC CONCENTRATION: 5 geographic terms (kaqa, qaqa, piqa, paqa, waqa) in 8 text cords. PURE CADASTRAL document -- no non-geographic vocabulary present.

JURIDICAL-WEIGHT BOUNDARY HYPOTHESIS: GG (grey-green justice/identity color) at 14 percent of cord palette suggests boundaries with legal force rather than merely physical demarcation. First cadastral document where GG fraction exceeds the typical neutral-cadastre ratio.

evidence.test: GG fraction in cadastral vs juridical sub-corpora

v3_contribution.claim: MOVABLE MARKER at C53: OKR-attested 'tied loosely around main cord' = physical bookmark / navigation aid. Non-encoding physical feature distinct from knot-level data -- paralleled by UR005's red-thread markers and UR037's yellow-camelid markers.
QUAI BRANLY TRIO CO-CATALOGUE: UR188 + UR189 + UR192 at the same Paris museum. Acquisition-history co-location warrants joint analysis. UR188 is the wall-focused member; UR189 + UR192 may complement.

companions.note: QUAI BRANLY TRIO MEMBER. Co-catalogued at Musee du Quai Branly, Paris. Deep-read in Session Tier 2 next batch should clarify relationship.
QUAI BRANLY TRIO MEMBER. Co-catalogued with UR188 + UR189.
Arica Playa Miller cadastral (275 cords, full 6/6 signature). UR058A is the larger southern-coast cadastral; UR188 is the compact wall-focused central-coast variant.

Leymebamba 7-sector cadastral survey (442 cords, kaqa x4). UR016 has more kaqa occurrences in absolute terms but at 4/16 STRING = 25 percent; UR188 at 3/8 STRING = 37.5 percent exceeds the per-text-cord ratio.
Pachacamac cadastral site plan (siqa x5 pyramids with ramps). UR188 has no siqa (no ramps); its focus is wall-based rather than pyramid-based.
Paracas cadastral (321 cords, full 5-marker signature + llalla). UR188 lacks llalla but matches the core cadastral signature.
Musee de l'Homme Paris compact route-cadastre (40 cords, 4/4 cadastral). Another Paris-museum cadastral; UR188 at Quai Branly is the wall-focused Paris-museum peer to AS080 at Musee de l'Homme.
Incahuasi sparse event register with compressed paqa-taqa-kaqa formula. UR273A compresses; UR188 expands wall-focus.

word_reading.note: 8 STRING at 100 percent dict hit. All 5 geographic markers dictionary-stable. The 8-cord sample is small but the concentration pattern is unambiguous.

document_type.note: cadastral_survey strongly supported by 62.5 percent geographic STRING concentration + corpus-record kaqa density + huaca anchor + standard cadastral vocabulary. Sub-type: wall-focused central-coast cadastre.

narrative.note: The 'wall-defined territorial survey with juridical-weight boundaries' reading is internally coherent. GG at 14 percent suggests legal-weight boundary marking but is interpretive; the specific juridical-vs-descriptive interpretation requires cross-validation with another GG-heavy cadastre.

data_integrity.note: 100 percent dict hit. 4 empty cords (5 percent). OKR-preserved C53 movable-marker. No Ascher layer summarised. Provenance unknown limits external anchoring to the Quai Branly trio.

sections.title: Wall-Focused Geographic Frame
sections.reading: Three kaqa cords anchor the survey with wall-boundaries. qaqa x2 rocks + piqa summit + paqa east + waqa huaca complete the geographic vocabulary. 5 different geographic terms in 8 text cords = pure cadastral signature.

key_cords.note: CORPUS-RECORD WALL CONCENTRATION (37.5 percent of STRING)

sections.title: GG Juridical-Weight Boundaries
sections.reading: GG (grey-green justice/identity color) at 14 percent of the cord palette marks boundaries with LEGAL FORCE. The walls + rocks become juridically-weighted demarcation rather than merely physical description.

sections.title: Movable Marker at C53
sections.reading: OKR-attested physical feature: C53 'tied loosely around main cord' = movable bookmark / navigation aid. Not an encoded value but a physical annotation for retrieval.

key_cords.note: OKR: loose tie = physical bookmark / navigation aid

sections.title: Numerical Body + Closing
sections.reading: 66 INT cords carry the survey's quantitative data (areas, distances, tribute values). 16 clusters organise the survey into boundary sections with plot entries.

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). Cadastral vocabulary corpus analysis + Quai Branly trio.
Musee du Quai Branly, Paris -- UR188, UR189, UR192 catalog records.
OKR cord-note on UR188: C53 'tied loosely around main cord' (movable marker).
Bertonio, L. (1612). Vocabulario de la lengua Aymara -- kaqa (wall).
Cerron-Palomino, R. (2000). Linguistica Aymara: origenes e historia.
Zuidema, R. T. (1964). The Ceque System of Cuzco.

Compare UR189 + UR192 (Quai Branly trio companions), UR058A (Arica cadastral), UR016 (Leymebamba cadastral), UR1095 (Pachacamac cadastral), UR118 (Paracas cadastral), AS080 (Paris Musee de l'Homme compact route-cadastre), UR273A (compressed cadastral formula).
